title: MobX
objectives: Understand MobX via a real-life analogy
Know when MobX fits (and when Riverpod is simpler)

sections.markdown:
**Think of MobX as a spreadsheet you dropped into your app.**

- Cell A1 has a number (an `@observable` — 'value that anyone can watch').
- Cell B1 is `=A1*2` (a `@computed` — derived automatically; re-evaluates when A1 changes).
- You edit A1 through a **formula bar** (an `@action` — the only sanctioned way to mutate values).
- Any chart connected to B1 auto-refreshes when A1 → B1 → chart cascades.

**MobX brings this reactive-spreadsheet model to Flutter.** You mark data as observable, mark methods that mutate it as actions, and the framework's dependency tracker figures out what needs to re-run when. Widgets wrapped in `Observer(() => …)` are the 'charts' — they rebuild when any observable they read changes.

**Why teams pick MobX:** developers coming from Vue/Angular/MobX-React find it immediately familiar. The reactivity model is genuinely elegant — no ceremony of events + reducers, no `.watch/read` API to memorize. Just read observables in a builder; they'll update.

**Trade-off:** requires code generation (`build_runner`), which slows the dev loop. Community is smaller than Riverpod's or Bloc's, so ecosystem support is thinner.

**MobX** is a transparent reactive state library from the JS/TS ecosystem, ported to Dart via `mobx` + `mobx_codegen`. You declare `@observable` fields, `@computed` getters, and `@action` methods on a store; wrap widgets in `Observer` to auto-rebuild when the observables they read change. Requires `build_runner` for the code-gen (`*.g.dart` files).
## The three annotations

rows: Marks a field as reactive. Reads are tracked; writes trigger reactions.
A derived value automatically recomputed when observables change.
The sanctioned way to mutate observables. Batches changes into one reaction.

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — counter with MobX
## Reactions — automatic side effects
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Mutating outside `@action`.** MobX warns you when observables are modified outside an action. Wrap mutations in `@action` methods or `runInAction(() => ...)` for one-offs.

**Mistake 2: Forgetting to run `build_runner`.** Every time you add/change observable fields, regenerate: `dart run build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs`. Otherwise you'll get compile errors about missing `_$CounterStore` mixin.
**Mistake 3: `Observer` around a huge subtree.** It rebuilds on any observable read. Move `Observer` DOWN to the smallest widget that actually cares.
**Mistake 4: Not disposing reactions.** `autorun`, `reaction`, `when` all return a dispose function — call it in your widget's `dispose()`. Otherwise memory leaks pile up.
**Mistake 5: Global `final store = Store()` in production.** Fine for tutorials; in real apps, inject via GetIt or provider so tests can swap it out.
## Best practices
**1. Use `@computed` for derived values.** Automatic caching + only re-evaluates when its inputs change.
**2. Group mutations in `@action`.** All writes inside one action → one reaction cycle instead of many.
**3. Small `Observer` wrappers.** One widget = one Observer; avoid one giant Observer around the whole screen.
**4. Dispose reactions.** Every `autorun`/`reaction`/`when` returns a dispose function; call it.
**5. Inject the store via DI (GetIt/Provider).** Not global final. Testable.
**6. For new apps in 2026, consider Riverpod first.** MobX's reactivity is elegant, but Riverpod delivers similar ergonomics without codegen.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — CounterStore.** Build the sample; add `decrement` and observe `doubled` re-computing.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — TodoStore.** `@observable` list of todos; `@computed` counts (total, completed); `@action` add/remove/toggle.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Async @action.** Use `@action` on an async fetch that sets `loading`, `data`, `error` observables.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — `reaction` for side effects.** Toast whenever a `count` observable exceeds 10, disposed properly.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Migrate a MobX screen to Riverpod.** Compare LOC, codegen overhead, dev-loop speed.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is MobX?**

**A:** MobX is a **transparent reactive state library** ported from JS/TS. You declare **`@observable`** fields (values you can watch), **`@computed`** getters (derived values that auto-recompute), and **`@action`** methods (the sanctioned way to mutate observables). Widgets wrapped in `Observer(builder: (_) => ...)` auto-rebuild when any observable they read inside the builder changes. Requires code generation via `build_runner` + `mobx_codegen`. The mental model is a reactive spreadsheet: change one cell, dependents refresh automatically. Popular with teams from Vue/Angular/MobX-React backgrounds who find the reactivity familiar.

**Q2: `@observable` vs `@computed` vs `@action`?**

**A:** **`@observable`** — a mutable field that MobX tracks. Reads register a dependency; writes trigger reactions. Only meaningful with an `@action`-wrapped mutation. **`@computed`** — a derived value expressed as a getter. MobX caches it and re-evaluates only when the observables it depends on change. Think of it as a smart cache. **`@action`** — a method that mutates observables. Wrapping mutations in `@action` batches related writes into a single reaction cycle (huge perf benefit) and enforces the 'writes only through actions' invariant. **Rule:** state → `@observable`, derived → `@computed`, mutation → `@action`.

**Q3: How does `Observer` know what to rebuild?**

**A:** When `Observer(builder: (_) => ...)`'s builder runs, MobX's tracker records EVERY `@observable` field and `@computed` getter accessed during that call. It builds a dependency graph. Later, when any of those observables is mutated (via an `@action`), MobX marks the affected Observer widgets dirty and rebuilds them. **Result:** you don't manually declare 'I depend on X, Y, Z'; MobX figures it out from your code. **Trade-off:** the graph is invisible — if a widget is unexpectedly rebuilding too often, you have to reason about which observables it might be reading. In practice this is elegant and rarely surprising.

**Q4: What are `autorun`, `reaction`, and `when`?**

**A:** Programmatic reactions — side effects that run when observables change, outside of widget rendering. **`autorun((_) => …)`** runs immediately and whenever any observable it reads changes. Perfect for logging or persisting to disk. **`reaction((_) => selector, (val) => callback)`** — declaratively watch a specific projection; fires only when the projected value changes. More surgical than autorun. **`when((_) => predicate, () => callback)`** — one-shot: fires the callback the FIRST time the predicate becomes true, then auto-disposes. Great for 'wait until user logs in, then navigate'. **All three return a dispose function you must call** to prevent leaks.

**Q5: MobX vs Riverpod — key differences?**

**A:** Both are elegant reactive state libraries. **MobX** — automatic dependency tracking via observers reading observables; requires codegen. Familiar to Vue/Angular/JS-MobX teams. **Riverpod** — explicit dependency via `ref.watch(provider)`; no codegen required (though there's optional generation). Compile-safe, `AsyncValue` built-in, better test story via `ProviderContainer`. **My rule:** for a new Flutter app, Riverpod's ergonomics are close enough to MobX's without the codegen tax. For a team already skilled in MobX from other frameworks, MobX is fine. Don't rewrite working MobX code just because Riverpod is trendier — but for greenfield, Riverpod wins.

**Q6: What are common MobX pitfalls?**

**A:** **(1) Mutating outside an `@action`** — MobX warns; batch writes into one action for perf. **(2) Forgetting `build_runner`** — the `_$Store` mixin doesn't exist until you generate it. **(3) Huge `Observer` widgets** — every observable read anywhere in the builder triggers rebuild; keep Observers small. **(4) Leaking reactions** — `autorun` etc. return dispose functions; call them in `dispose()`. **(5) Global `final store = Store()`** — untestable; inject via DI. **(6) Nested observables** — a `List` of observables needs `ObservableList<T>`, not `List<T>`. Similarly `ObservableMap<K, V>` and `ObservableSet<T>`.

## Real-world usage

- **Teams from Vue/Angular/MobX-React** — familiar mental model.
- **Small-to-medium apps** where developers prefer implicit reactivity.
- **Wonderous** (a Google-showcase Flutter app) — uses MobX.
- **Some enterprise Alibaba apps** — MobX is popular in Chinese Flutter ecosystem.
- **Portfolios / educational content** — MobX's spreadsheet model is a great teaching aid.

## Advanced notes

- **`ObservableList<T>` / `ObservableMap<K, V>` / `ObservableSet<T>`** — reactive collections.
- **`ObservableFuture<T>` / `ObservableStream<T>`** — wrap async work; `status: pending / fulfilled / rejected`.
- **`ObservableStream.map(...)`** — reactive stream operators.
- **`runInAction(() => …)`** — one-off action wrapper (no `@action` annotation needed).
- **`Store` mixin** — the base for stores; provides tracking machinery.
- **`mobx_codegen`** — the code generator; regenerate on every observable change.
- **`equatable` on stores** — not typical; MobX stores are usually mutable identity-based.
- **`GetIt` for DI** — the common pairing for MobX injection.
- **`asyncAction`** — modern replacement for wrapping `Future` bodies.
- **`mobxSideEffectRunner`** — hook side effects into widget lifecycle.

flashcards.back: Observer(builder: (_) => …) — auto-tracks reads, rebuilds on changes.
autorun, reaction, when — all return dispose functions.
build_runner + mobx_codegen — regenerate on every observable change.
MobX = automatic dep tracking + codegen. Riverpod = explicit watch + no codegen + compile-safe.

challenges.text: Observe with Observer widget; watch the doubled @computed update automatically.
Async @action loading a user; show status via ObservableFuture.
reaction to show a snackbar when count > 10; dispose properly.
Inject the store via GetIt; write a test that swaps it with a fake.
Migrate a small MobX screen to Riverpod; compare LOC + codegen overhead.

interviewQuestions: How does Observer know what to rebuild?
What are autorun, reaction, and when?
